questions.specification: A rugby player doubles the net force applied during a tackle while the mass remains constant. What happens to the acceleration?

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question applies Newton’s second law (F = ma) to predict how acceleration changes when net force doubles while mass stays constant, which is covered under Newton's laws of motion in linear contexts.

options.markscheme: If the force were halved ($F' = \frac{1}{2}F$), then with constant mass $a' = \frac{F'}{m} = \frac{1}{2}a$; but the force is doubled, not halved.
If mass is constant, $a = \frac{F}{m}$. Doubling $F$ changes $a$, so it cannot remain constant.
Using $F = ma$, with constant $m$, $a = \frac{F}{m}$. If $F$ doubles to $2F$, then $a$ doubles to $2a$.

options.content: It increases but by less than double
options.markscheme: With constant mass, $a$ is directly proportional to $F$ ($a = \frac{F}{m}$), so doubling $F$ produces exactly double the acceleration, not less.

questions.specification: When a footballer kicks a ball and the ball pushes back on their foot, this is an example of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes an action–reaction force pair (foot pushes ball, ball pushes back), which is Newton’s Third Law; this falls under Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts.

options.markscheme: Newton's First Law (the Law of Inertia) states that an object will remain at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net external force. It does not describe interaction pairs between two objects.

Newton's Second Law relates the net force acting on an object to its mass and acceleration ($F = ma$). While it governs the motion of the ball and foot separately, it is not the primary law describing the mutual interaction forces.

Newton's Third Law states that for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. When the foot exerts a force on the ball, the ball simultaneously exerts a force of equal magnitude but opposite direction on the foot. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation describes the attractive force between two masses. It is not the law used to explain the equal-and-opposite contact forces between a foot and a ball during a kick.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/fd442cf7-c2f6-45cc-870f-b695048562fe.png)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.4 Work, energy, and power in force application (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.4 Work, energy, and power in force application (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which athlete did more mechanical work over a distance using a force–distance graph; work is the area under an F–d graph, which falls under work, energy, and power in force application.

options.content: Athlete A, because Athlete A applied a greater force at the beginning of the motion
options.markscheme: While Athlete A applied more force initially, mechanical work is determined by the total area under the force–distance graph, not just the initial force.

options.content: Athlete B, because Athlete B applied a greater force at the end of the motion
options.markscheme: While Athlete B applied more force towards the end, mechanical work depends on the total area under the force–distance graph.

options.content: Both athletes performed the same amount of work because the areas under their force–distance graphs are equal
options.markscheme:
Correct answer: C. Mechanical work $W$ is equal to the area under the force–distance graph ($W = \int F\,ds$).
For Athlete A: $\text{Area}=(15\times 5)+\frac{1}{2}(5\times 15)=75+37.5=112.5\text{ J}$.
For Athlete B: $\text{Area}=\frac{1}{2}(5\times 15)+(15\times 5)=37.5+75=112.5\text{ J}$.
Since the areas are identical, both performed the same amount of work.

options.content: Athlete A, because Athlete A maintained a constant force for a longer distance than Athlete B
options.markscheme: Both athletes maintained a force of $15\text{ N}$ for a distance of $5\text{ m}$. This statement is incorrect.

questions.specification: A gymnast performs a landing on a high-impact mat. At the instant of contact, the gymnast exerts a downward force of $2200\text{ N}$ on the mat. According to Newton’s third law of motion, which statement describes the force exerted by the mat on the gymnast?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about Newton’s third law (action–reaction force pair) in a landing scenario, focusing on equal and opposite forces between gymnast and mat.

options.content: The mat exerts an upward force of $2200\text{ N}$ on the gymnast.
options.markscheme: Correct: by Newton’s third law, the reaction force is equal in magnitude ($2200\text{ N}$) and opposite in direction (upward) to the force applied by the gymnast (downward).

options.content: The mat exerts a downward force of $2200\text{ N}$ on the gymnast.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the reaction force acts in the opposite direction to the action force, so it would be upward, not downward.

options.content: The mat exerts an upward force equal to the gymnast's weight only.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the contact (normal) force during landing is not necessarily equal to body weight; it can be greater or less depending on deceleration.

options.content: The mat exerts an upward force of less than $2200\text{ N}$ due to the compression of the foam.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: at that instant, Newton’s third law requires the mat’s force on the gymnast to match the gymnast’s force on the mat in magnitude ($2200\text{ N}$), regardless of compression.

options.markscheme: Newton's third law describes the equal and opposite forces during the collision, but the situation described (an isolated system with equal masses swapping speeds) is best used to demonstrate conservation of momentum rather than the force-pair law.

With negligible external horizontal forces, total momentum is conserved. Before: $p = mv + 0 = mv$. After: $p = 0 + mv = mv$. Since total momentum is unchanged, this demonstrates conservation of momentum.
Kinetic energy is not necessarily conserved in all collisions; the key statement supported by the given speed swap under negligible external forces is conservation of momentum.
Newton's first law concerns motion with no net external force on a single object, not the conservation principle governing a two-cart collision.

questions.specification: Which factor determines the frictional force between a sprinter’s shoe and the track?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.3 Role of friction in motion and its impact on performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.3 Role of friction in motion and its impact on performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what most influences friction between a sprinter’s shoe and the track; this directly tests understanding of friction and the coefficient of friction in motion/performance.

options.content: The normal reaction force between the shoe and the track
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The frictional force depends on the normal reaction force $N$ (via $F_f=\mu N$), but this is not the most appropriate single factor if only one must be chosen.

options.content: The coefficient of friction between the shoe surface and the track
options.markscheme: Correct. The frictional force is determined by $F_f=\mu N$, so the coefficient of friction $\mu$ is a key determining factor (along with $N$).

options.content: The surface roughness/texture of the track
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Track texture can affect friction by changing the effective coefficient of friction, but it is not the direct determining factor in the friction equation compared with $\mu$.

options.content: The contact area of the shoe with the track
options.markscheme: Incorrect. For dry friction in the basic model, friction is approximately independent of contact area; it depends mainly on $\mu$ and $N$.

questions.specification: Two ice hockey players collide. One has a momentum of $100\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$, and the other has a momentum of $80\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ in the opposite direction. Take the $100\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ player’s direction as forward (positive). What is the magnitude and direction of the total momentum of the system immediately after the collision if they stick together (ignore external forces)?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.2 Momentum changes and collisions in sports scenarios (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.2 Momentum changes and collisions in sports scenarios (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about conservation of momentum in a collision where two players stick together (perfectly inelastic collision), requiring calculation of total system momentum and its direction.

options.markscheme:
Total momentum is conserved in a closed system. Let the direction of the first player ($100\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$) be forward (positive).

$p_{\text{total}} = p_1 + p_2 = 100\text{ kg m s}^{-1} + (-80\text{ kg m s}^{-1}) = 20\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ in the forward direction.

Since momentum is conserved, the momentum immediately after the collision remains $20\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ forward.
This incorrectly adds the magnitudes of the momenta without considering their opposite directions.
This correctly identifies the magnitude but assigns the wrong direction. The net momentum is in the direction of the player with the greater initial momentum.
This incorrectly adds the magnitudes and assigns the wrong direction.

questions.specification: A $70\text{ kg}$ basketball player jumps and generates an upward force of $2100\text{ N}$. What is their upward acceleration? (Take $g = 10\text{ m s}^{-2}$)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.2 Forces, motion and movement > B.2.1 Newton’s laws of motion > B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.2.1.1 Newton's laws of motion in linear and angular contexts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires using Newton’s second law (net force = ma) with weight (mg) to find acceleration during a jump, which is an application of Newton's laws in linear motion.

options.markscheme: This value would result if the net force were $700\text{ N}$ or if an error was made in the weight calculation.

The weight of the player is $W = mg = 70\text{ kg} \times 10\text{ m s}^{-2} = 700\text{ N}$. 
According to Newton's Second Law, the net force $F_{\text{net}}$ is the difference between the upward force generated and the player's weight: 
$F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{upward}} - W = 2100\text{ N} - 700\text{ N} = 1400\text{ N}$.
The acceleration $a$ is then: 
$a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \frac{1400\text{ N}}{70\text{ kg}} = 20\text{ m s}^{-2}$.
Incorrect calculation.
This value would be correct if the upward force generated was $1050\text{ N}$ ($F_{\text{net}} = 350\text{ N}$).

options.content: Work, because an upward force is applied over a vertical displacement.
options.markscheme: The lifter applies an upward force and the barbell moves upward, so a force acts through a displacement (mechanical work is done: $W = Fs\cos\theta$ with $\theta = 0$).

options.content: Not work, because the muscles are contracting.
options.markscheme: This confuses physiological effort with mechanical work. In mechanics, work requires a force to act through a displacement.

options.content: Momentum, because the velocity is changing.
options.markscheme: Changing velocity indicates acceleration (Newton’s second law) and a change in momentum, but the action of lifting through a displacement against a force is classified as doing work.

options.content: Friction, because friction opposes the motion.
options.markscheme: Friction is a resistive force that can oppose motion, but the primary mechanical classification of lifting the barbell upward is that work is done on it by the applied force.

The frictional force $F_f$ is directly proportional to the coefficient of friction, given by the relationship $F_f = \mu R$.
The frictional force $F_f$ is directly proportional to the normal reaction force $R$, given by the relationship $F_f = \mu R$.
The mass of the player's shoes has no impact on the interaction or forces between the basketball and the court surface.

options.markscheme: Using $F=ma$, for block $P$: $F=4.0\times 3.0=12\ \text{N}$. For block $Q$: $a=F/m=12/2.0=6.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$, so $1.5\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$ is incorrect.
Using $F=ma$, for block $P$: $F=4.0\times 3.0=12\ \text{N}$. For block $Q$: $a=12/2.0=6.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$, so $3.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$ is incorrect.
For block $P$, the net force is $F=ma=(4.0)(3.0)=12\ \text{N}$. The same net force acts on $Q$, so $a_Q=F/m=12/2.0=6.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$ (correct option: C).
Using $F=ma$, for block $P$: $F=4.0\times 3.0=12\ \text{N}$. For block $Q$: $a=12/2.0=6.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$, so $12.0\text{ m}\ s^{-2}$ is incorrect.
